Title: Pro-Net widens EV retail network amid strong e.Mas demand
Article:
PRO-Net, the sales and distribution arm for Proton Holdings Bhd's new energy vehicle business, is expanding its retail network following strong sales growth for the Proton e.Mas brand in the first four months of 2026.
The company said Proton e.Mas deliveries surged 329.5 per cent year-on-year to 11,617 units between January and April, supported by demand for its electric vehicle (EV) and plug-in hybrid models.
As part of the expansion, Pro-Net has opened two new Proton e.Mas outlets this month.
One is located along Jalan Sultan Ismail in Kuala Lumpur alongside a Proton internal combustion engine (ICE) showroom, while the other is at Pavilion Bukit Jalil.
The new outlets also introduce Proton e.Mas' updated corporate identity, featuring redesigned showrooms with digital displays and upgraded customer service areas.
Pro-Net currently operates 53 sales outlets nationwide, supported by around 1,600 employees across sales, servicing and administrative functions.
"The rapid growth of Proton e.Mad in Malaysia is driven not only by the strength of our product lineup, but also by the confidence customers have in our sales and aftersales ecosystem," said Pro-Net chief executive officer Zhang Qiang.
"By establishing a strong support structure alongside every model launch, we can give customers greater reassurance about the long-term ownership experience while strengthening brand loyalty and advocacy.
"We target to continue expanding our outlet network and as we do so, Proton e.Mad will be able to reach more potential EV buyers while delivering a higher standard of customer care and convenience," he added.
The expansion comes as competition in Malaysia's EV market intensifies, with consumers placing greater emphasis on charging access, aftersales support and overall ownership experience.
Pro-Net said the updated showroom identity will be rolled out progressively across existing and future Proton e.Mas outlets nationwide.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police arrest man over viral schoolgirl molest allegations
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Police have arrested a 28-year-old man following a viral social media post alleging the molest of a schoolgirl in Seri Alam.
Acting Johor police chief Deputy Commissioner Hoo Chuan Huat said police detected the viral post uploaded on Facebook by the account "Mohd Fadli Salleh" yesterday.
"Following this, police received a report at 9pm yesterday from the victim's 37-year-old mother, who said the incident occurred last year in the Seri Alam area" he said in a statement today.
Hoo said acting on information, a team from the Seri Alam district police headquarters Criminal Investigation Department arrested the suspect at 10.30pm yesterday.
A preliminary urine screening on the suspect returned negative for drugs, and checks showed he has no prior criminal record.
Hoo added that a remand application against the suspect under Section 117 of the Criminal Procedure Code would be made at the Johor Baru court today.
It was
reported
yesterday that police were investigating allegations involving a primary school teacher in Masai following claims that he had allegedly returned to teaching duties despite multiple reports lodged over the case.
The allegations, which surfaced widely on social media, claimed that reports had been lodged with police, education authorities and the Public Complaints Bureau (Sispaa) since last year.
The postings alleged that the victim, a 12-year-old pupil, had admitted to school authorities that she was involved in a sexual relationship with a male teacher at the school.
The teacher, said to be in his late 20s, was allegedly reassigned temporarily before later returning to active teaching duties.
The claims triggered public concern, particularly among parents, after allegations surfaced that the teacher continued teaching at a primary school in Pasir Gudang.
The case is being investigated under Section 14(a) of the Sexual Offences Against Children Act 2017 for touching any part of a child's body, which carries a penalty of up to 20 years' imprisonment and whipping upon conviction.
Those with information can contact the nearest police station, the Johor police hotline at 019-2792095, or the Johor police contingent headquarters operations room at 07-2212999.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dayang upgrade to 'Buy' with higher target price after Q1 results beat expectations
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: CIMB Securities Sdn Bhd has upgraded Dayang Enterprise Holdings Bhd to "Buy" from "Hold", citing improving upstream activity, stronger vessel utilisation and potential contract wins that could drive earnings growth.
The firm also set a higher target price of RM2.15 from RM1.70 previously to reflect an upward revision to financial year 2027 (FY27) earnings forecast.
Dayang's first-quarter core net profit of RM23 million exceeded CIMB Securities' expectations, accounting for 14 per cent of its previous full-year forecast, while broadly matching consensus estimates at 11.7 per cent.
The upbeat performance was mainly driven by a sharp improvement in profitability, with the company's earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortisation (Ebitda) margin expanding 11.8 percentage points to 35.7 per cent.
CIMB Securities said the company's vessel utilisation rate rose to 45 per cent in the first quarter (Q1) from 26 per cent a year earlier, helping narrow losses in its marine charter segment.
"The marine charter segment's losses before interest and tax narrowed to RM15.3 million from RM23.3 million in Q1 of 2025, supported by better vessel utilisation," it said in a note today.
However, Dayang's topline softened, with revenue declining 14 per cent year-on-year to RM132 million due to fewer topside maintenance work orders during the quarter.
The company's topside maintenance earnings before interest and tax remained broadly stable at RM35.5 million, down marginally by two per cent year-on-year.
Dayang declared a first interim dividend of seven sen per share, unchanged from the corresponding quarter last year.
CIMB Securities said maintenance, construction and modification activities are expected to remain resilient amid elevated crude oil prices, as operators continue prioritising production uptime.
Brent crude was trading at about US$110 per barrel as of May 21, 2026.
"However, higher logistics costs may increase the group's operating expenses.
"The company continues to actively participate in tenders, with involvement in more than 24 projects as of April 2026," it added.
Potential catalysts include securing Petronas Carigali decommissioning jobs worth an estimated RM2 billion to RM3 billion, a two-year transport and installation contract valued at around RM300 million, as well as turnaround works at the Pengerang Integrated Complex.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Bad timing': Trump says Iran war could keep him from son's wedding
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump said Thursday he would try to make it to his eldest son's wedding but that it was "bad timing" because of the Iran war.
Donald Trump Jr, 48, is set to tie the knot for the second time this weekend, marrying Bettina Anderson in the Bahamas, US media reported.
But at the same time Trump is working on a deal to end his unpopular war with Iran, which has sunk the president's poll ratings and sparked voter anger over the cost of living ahead of November's midterm elections.
"He'd like me to go. It's going to be just a small little private affair and I'm going to try and make it," Trump told reporters in the Oval Office when asked if he would attend his son's wedding.
"I said, 'This is not good timing for me. I have a thing called Iran and other things.'"
Trump said he was conscious of how the media would cover it if he did go to the wedding in the middle of a war that remains stuck in a stalemate.
"That's one I can't win on. If I do attend, I get killed. If I don't attend, I get killed – by the fake news of course," Trump said.
"Hopefully they're going to have a great marriage."
Known as Don Jr, the president's son is an executive vice president at The Trump Organisation, the family's luxury real estate conglomerate, and a vocal proponent of his father's right-wing MAGA political agenda.
He is often seen at his father's side, including earlier this month when they returned to the White House together from Trump senior's Mar-a-Lago resort in Florida.
Last year Don Jr said he could run for president "maybe one day." His father's second and final term runs until January, 2029 and the Republican president has not yet anointed a successor.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Three dead after car crashes into cement lorry on Karak Highway
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Three men were killed while a woman was seriously injured after the car they were travelling in crashed into the rear of a cement lorry at Km34 of the Kuala Lumpur-bound Karak Highway near Kampung Sungai Chinchin early today.
Selangor Fire and Rescue Department said the crash, involving a Volkswagen car and a cement lorry, was reported at 2.07am.
"Firefighters from the Selayang fire station arrived at the scene at 2.35am.
"Four victims were trapped in the vehicle. The 27-year-old woman suffered severe head injuries and a fractured left leg," the statement said.
It added that the three men, believed to be in their 20s, were pronounced dead at the scene by Health Ministry personnel.
All victims were extricated by firefighters before being handed over to medical authorities for further action.
A total of six firefighters, assisted by a fire rescue tender and an Emergency Medical Rescue Services (EMRS) vehicle, were involved in the operation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit opens higher against greenback, major currencies
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit opened firmer against the US dollar and major currencies on Friday, supported by easing geopolitical tensions in Middle East and Malaysia's improving macroeconomic outlook.
At 8am, the local currency strengthened to 3.9550/9645 against the greenback from Thursday's close of 3.9595/9630.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said optimism that the United States (US) and Iran would soon reach an amicable resolution continues to build.
He said West Texas Intermediate (WTI) and Brent crude prices fell 1.49 per cent and 2.32 per cent to US$97.79 per barrel and US$102.58 per barrel, respectively.
"According to the Iranian news agency, Tehran is evaluating the proposal from the US, which has narrowed the gaps to some extent," he told Bernama.
He also said the ringgit should remain in a better position amid Malaysia's positive macroeconomic backdrop, particularly as the current account surplus rose to 3.0 per cent of gross domestic product (GDP) in the first quarter of 2026 from 0.5 per cent in the previous quarter.
"Apart from that, the government's fiscal position continued to improve, with the fiscal deficit narrowing to RM17.1 billion or 3.3 per cent of GDP in the first quarter of 2026 from RM21.9 billion or 4.5 per cent of GDP in the corresponding period of 2025
"Hence, the ringgit is expected to remain well supported at around RM3.95 to RM3.96 against the US dollar today," he said.
At the opening, the ringgit traded higher against a basket of major currencies.
The local currency appreciated against the British pound to 5.3120/3247 from 5.3220/3267 at Thursday's close, strengthened against the euro to 4.5945/6056 from 4.6037/6078 previously, and rose against the Japanese yen to 2.4866/4928 from 2.4906/4929.
The ringgit also traded higher against regional currencies.
It strengthened against the Singapore dollar to 3.0949/1026 from 3.0967/0997 previously and appreciated against the Thai baht to 12.1286/1648 from 12.1304/1468.
The local note also improved against the Indonesian rupiah to 223.8/224.5 from 224.1/224.4 previously and appreciated against the Philippine peso to 6.42/6.44 from 6.43/6.44 previously.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysians detained in flotilla mission arrive safely in Istanbul
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: All 29 Malaysians detained by Israel in international waters while participating in the Global Sumud Flotilla (GSF) 2.0 humanitarian mission have arrived safely in Istanbul, Turkiye, at 5.45pm local time yesterday.
"So far, all of them are in good condition although six participants, including five men, sustained injuries and require medical examinations," Foreign Minister Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan said in a brief statement to BH today.
It was reported last night that all 428 humanitarian activists involved in the GSF 2.0 mission, including the 29 Malaysians detained by Israel in international waters,
had been released
.
Sumud Nusantara Command Center (SNCC) director-general Datuk Dr Sani Araby Abdul Alim Araby was reported as saying that SNCC had been informed by Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim that Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan had dispatched three Turkish Airlines aircraft to Ramon Airport.
SNCC confirmed the latest reports that all 428 flotilla activists were being transported by bus to Ramon Airport.
They were expected to arrive in Istanbul on three specially chartered flights sponsored by the Turkish government, bearing flight codes TK6919, TK6921 and TK6925.
They are expected to arrive at about 10pm Malaysian time and the activists would be received by officials from the Malaysian Embassy in Turkiye, Wisma Putra and the Turkish government.
The Malaysian delegation is expected to undergo medical examinations at hospitals in Turkiye following reports that some detainees had been assaulted and suffered injuries.
SNCC expects the Malaysian delegation to depart for home tomorrow and arrive at Kuala Lumpur International Airport on Sunday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Form Six student killed in three-vehicle collision
Article:
SABAK BERNAM: A Form Six student was killed in a three-vehicle collision at Jalan Kampung Tengah here yesterday.
In the 6pm incident, the victim, a student at Kolej Tingkatan Enam Tunku Abdul Rahman Putra, died at the scene.
District police chief Superintendent Md Yusof Ahmad said the accident involved two motorcycles and a van.
The incident occurred when one motorcycle rammed into the rear of the other motorcycle, which was slowing down to turn into a junction on the left side of the road.
"The impact caused the victim to lose control of the motorcycle, veer into the opposite lane, and collide with the oncoming van," he said when contacted last night.
Yusof said the motorcyclist fell and sustained severe head injuries before being pronounced dead.
The case is being investigated under Section 41 (1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.
Those with information can contact Inspector Mohd Shaifuddin Mohd Nor at 017-6645812 to assist in the investigation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump 'very disappointed' with Nato allies over Iran war stance, Rubio says
Article:
HELSINGBORG, Sweden: US Secretary of State Marco Rubio will meet Nato allies on Friday for the first time since President Donald Trump called the alliance into question due to divisions over the Iran war and Washington announced plans to pull 5,000 troops from Europe.
Before leaving for a meeting of Nato foreign ministers in Sweden, Rubio said Trump was "very disappointed" in alliance members who had not allowed the US to use bases on their territory for the war, singling out Spain in particular.
"You have countries like Spain denying us the use of these bases - well then why are you in Nato? That's a very fair question," Rubio told reporters in Miami. "In fairness, other countries in Nato have been very helpful. But we need to discuss that."
Trump has fiercely criticised Nato members for not doing more to help the US-Israeli military campaign. He has said he is considering withdrawing from the alliance and questioned whether Washington was bound to honour its mutual defence pact.
Nato officials have stressed the US did not ask the 32-member alliance to take part in the Iran war, but many members have honoured commitments to allow US forces to use their airspace and bases on their territory.
European concerns about Trump's attitude toward Nato were also exacerbated this year by Trump's push to acquire Greenland, a territory of fellow Nato member Denmark.
EUROPEANS SAY THEY ARE READY TO HELP WITH HORMUZ
At the gathering in the Swedish town of Helsingborg, European ministers are expected to try to placate the US by stressing they are ready to help with freedom of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz when conditions permit and to take more responsibility for European security.
Iran has restricted traffic through the strait during the war.
European fears about the US president's commitment to Nato have been heightened by the decision to withdraw 5,000 troops from Europe, which US officials linked to German Chancellor Friedrich Merz's criticism of Trump's Iran war strategy.
Washington's allies have also been confused and unsettled by the way the decision was communicated.
US officials first said the troops would be withdrawn from Germany but later said they would delay the deployment of a brigade to Poland. The United States has also said a planned deployment of long-range Tomahawk missiles to Germany will no longer go ahead.
In addition, it plans to tell Nato allies it will shrink the pool of military capabilities the US makes available to the alliance in a crisis, three sources familiar with the matter told Reuters.
Nato's top commander, US Air Force General Alexus Grynkewich, sought this week to reassure European allies about the recent decisions, saying further drawdowns would stretch over years to give allies time to develop capabilities to replace them.
"As the European pillar of the alliance gets stronger, this allows the US to reduce its presence in Europe and limit itself to providing only those critical capabilities that allies cannot yet provide," he told reporters at Nato headquarters on Tuesday. --REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Genting Malaysia's Q1 earnings miss expectations
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Genting Malaysia Bhd's core earnings for the first quarter ended March 31, 2026, fell sharply to RM10 million, down 74 per cent quarter-on-quarter and 81 per cent year-on-year, missing market expectations.
Affin Hwang Investment Bank Bhd said the results accounted for only two per cent of its full-year forecast and one per cent of consensus estimates.
The weaker performance was mainly due to higher operating costs in both the United States and United Kingdom, the firm added.
In the US, colder weather led to higher utility expenses, while the opening of a new casino in New York also resulted in upfront costs.
Higher finance costs further weighed on earnings, Affin Hwang said, adding that the pace of revenue recovery will need to improve significantly.
The firm said in Malaysia, visitor numbers at Resorts World Genting fell four per cent year-on-year to 5.5 million, partly due to fewer school holidays.
Domestic visitors made up 68 per cent of total footfall, while foreign visitors mainly came from Singapore, China, India and Indonesia. International visitor numbers still rose four per cent year-on-year.
While Visit Malaysia 2026 could help improve visitor traffic next year, Affin Hwang does not expect it to provide a major boost to the stock as Genting Malaysia's earnings remain heavily dependent on its gaming business.
Theme park ticket sales also declined six per cent year-on-year, as the previous year benefited from promotional ticket pricing.
In the US, management did not provide guidance on near-term margins for the newly opened New York casino, citing its early operating stage.
However, the group expects long-term gross margins to exceed 20 per cent.
Affin Hwang maintained a "Hold" call on the stock but lower its sum-of-parts target price to RM2.05.
Its earnings forecasts for FY2026 to FY2028 have been cut by between five per cent and 22 per cent to reflect a slower recovery.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 9 to be charged over Bukit Bintang publicity stunt
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Nine people are expected to be charged in court today following a viral publicity stunt involving flares and reckless driving to promote a clothing store in Bukit Bintang.
Police said six others will be released and serve as prosecution witnesses.
The suspects were part of a group of 15, including 14 men and a woman aged between 17 and 30, who were detained less than 24 hours after a video of
the incident
surfaced on social media.
The arrests were carried out between 12pm and 10pm in Batu Caves, Shah Alam, Kajang and Dengkil following public tip-offs.
The group consisted of the 24-year-old shop owner, employees and individuals hired to perform the stunt. Eight of them were paid between RM50 and RM700 to take part in the activity.
Preliminary investigations found that the stunt was carried out to promote a clothing business owned by one of the suspects, linked to a promotional campaign marking the outlet's sixth anniversary in Dengkil.
The police were earlier tracking the group after they ignited flares while riding in the back of a pickup truck along Jalan Sultan Ismail, near a fast-food restaurant in Bukit Bintang, early on Sunday.
Police also seized a pickup truck, two electric scrambler motorcycles and clothing worn during the incident.
Investigations were carried out under Section 290 of the Penal Code for public nuisance, Section 14 of the Minor Offences Act for indecent behaviour, Section 6 of the Explosives Act 1957, Section 42 of the Road Transport Act for reckless and dangerous driving, and Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Texas sues Meta, WhatsApp over encryption privacy claims
Article:
NEW YORK: The Texas Attorney General's Office on Thursday sued WhatsApp and its parent company Meta Platforms, alleging they misled consumers about the strength and scope of WhatsApp's encryption measures, which a Meta spokesman denied.
The lawsuit filed in Harrison County court said WhatsApp and Meta falsely assured users that WhatsApp messages are encrypted despite having access to "virtually all" private communications on the messaging app.
"WhatsApp markets its services as secure and encrypted, but it does not deliver on those promises," Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton said in a statement.
Meta spokesman Andy Stone said on social media that the lawsuit's allegations are false and that WhatsApp cannot access people's encrypted communications.
The lawsuit seeks a court order barring Meta and WhatsApp from accessing Texans' WhatsApp messages without their consent, as well as monetary penalties.
Texas' lawsuit cites news reports about a federal investigation into claims that Meta had access to unencrypted WhatsApp messages and a whistleblower report to the US Securities and Exchange Commission.
The lawsuit was filed under the Texas Deceptive Trade Practices Act, the state's main consumer protection law.
Paxton's office has filed a number of similar data privacy lawsuits against major companies, including Google, which agreed in May 2025 to pay US$1.375 billion to settle claims that it violated users' data privacy.
On May 11, Paxton's office filed a lawsuit accusing Netflix of spying on children and other consumers by collecting their data without consent and designing its platform to be addictive.
Netflix denied the allegations and said the lawsuit is based on inaccurate and distorted information.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Wall Street banks boost Warner Bros loan to over US$10bil ahead of Paramount merger
Article:
NEW YORK: Wall Street banks led by JPMorgan have increased the size of a loan package for Warner Bros Discovery to more than US$10 billion as the media company seeks to refinance debt ahead of its planned merger with Paramount Skydance, according to terms seen by Reuters on Thursday.
The US dollar term loan was increased to US$9 billion from US$5 billion, while a one billion euro (US$1.16 billion) loan remained unchanged, the terms showed.
Barclays, BNP Paribas, Deutsche Bank, NatWest, RBC, UBS, Wells Fargo and Goldman Sachs are the bookrunners on the transaction, according to the terms.
The financing comes as Paramount moves ahead with its planned acquisition of Warner Bros Discovery, a deal valued at about US$110 billion that was signed recently after Netflix declined to raise its offer.
The merger would combine Paramount assets, including CBS, MTV, Comedy Central and BET with Warner Bros Discovery properties such as CNN, TNT and Food Network.
JPMorgan has already earned US$189 million in financing and other fees tied to Warner Bros-related transactions, according to a Reuters report in January.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US stocks end volatile session higher as oil prices retreat
Article:
NEW YORK: Wall Street stocks finished mostly higher on Thursday following a choppy session as markets tried to parse the prospects for a Middle East peace deal while digesting earnings from Nvidia and Walmart.
After oil prices ascended for the first part of the day, benchmark futures contracts reversed course and finished down about two per cent.
International Energy Agency chief Fatih Birol warned that the world may be entering "the red zone" on oil supplies, but markets continued to welcome signs — even if vague or faint — that a deal between Washington and Iran could be forthcoming.
US Secretary of State Marco Rubio voiced hope of progress on ending the war with Iran, with mediator Pakistan's army chief due to arrive in the Islamic republic for talks.
"I believe the Pakistanis will be travelling to Tehran today. So hopefully that'll advance this further," Rubio told reporters on Thursday.
Major US indices finished modestly higher after starting in the red, with the broad-based S&P 500 ending up 0.2 per cent.
"The market still shows its muscles here. It's very resilient," said Peter Cardillo of Spartan Capital Securities. "And, of course, is betting on the fact that the war is about to end."
There were earlier big gains for technology stocks in Asia after chip giant Nvidia posted record quarterly revenue of US$81.6 billion, blowing past analyst forecasts on the voracious demand for artificial intelligence hardware.
Sentiment was also boosted by Elon Musk's filing for a public sale of SpaceX shares, which could be the largest initial public offering in history as the rocket and satellite company seeks to raise up to US$75 billion.
"This could be a blockbuster summer for IPOs with OpenAI also expected to list in the coming weeks," said Kathleen Brooks, research director at XTB.
"How the market absorbs these new listings will be crucial for the future of the AI trade, as both companies are at the heart of the AI revolution," she said.
South Korea's benchmark Kospi surged 8.4 per cent, helped by Samsung Electronics shares after unions paused an 18-day strike.
Japan's Nikkei ended with a gain of 3.1 per cent.
But the positive sentiment dissipated in European trading, where stocks ended mostly lower.
Despite Nvidia's profit growth, the company's shares failed to get a boost as they have in previous quarters. They initially edged higher but finished down 1.8 per cent.
Also lower was Walmart, which tumbled 7.3 per cent as traders fretted over the company's cautious forecast and commentary on consumers.
"The high-income customer is spending with confidence into many categories while the lower-income consumer is more budget conscious and perhaps navigating financial distress," said Walmart Chief Financial Officer John David Rainey.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: New York City offering US$50 World Cup tickets for residents
Article:
NEW YORK: New York City will offer its residents a chance to snag US$50 tickets to World Cup games in New Jersey this summer, Mayor Zohran Mamdani said, with 1,000 affordable tickets up for grabs through a lottery system.
The discounted tickets come with free round-trip bus transportation to the stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey and will be split across five group-stage and two knockout games, with 150 tickets available per match.
"We are making sure that working people will not be priced out of the game that they helped to create," Mamdani told a press conference in the city's Harlem neighbourhood, quipping that the US$50 price tag was the equivalent of five coffees in New York City.
Starting on May 25, residents will be able to enter a lottery for a chance to purchase the tickets, which will be non-transferable and distributed to winners at the bus boarding location in an effort to prevent scalping.
Ticket affordability has been a key issue for the tournament being co-hosted by the US, Canada and Mexico.
According to TicketData, a ticket price tracking platform, the average "get-in" cost for a group-stage match in New York as of Wednesday was US$864. Some resale tickets are listed for thousands of dollars across various platforms, including FIFA's official resale and exchange marketplace.
The best seats for the final on July 19 in New Jersey were put on sale for nearly US$33,000.
FIFA President Gianni Infantino defended high World Cup ticket prices this month.
"This is the moment to make sure that everybody is included, that everybody is lifted up," said New York City Councilman Yusef Salaam.
Winners of the lottery will be able to purchase up to two tickets each. --REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Inmates thankful for second chance
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
TAPAH: A prison inmate released under the Madani Yakin Licensed Prisoner Release (PBSL) programme described the opportunity as a blessing and a  second chance.
Hanum (not her real name), 25, from Seberang Perak, said returning to society through a job placement at a restaurant was a chance to prove that past mistakes would not be repeated.
The former welder, who is serving a sentence for offences related to concealing stolen property, under Sections 414 and 411 of the Penal Code, said seven months in jail had taught her discipline and spiritual resilience, through classes under the Human Development Programme.
"I want to change. In the past, I listened more to friends and disregarded my mother.
"Now my focus is to work throughout the three-month PBSL period and seek forgiveness from my mother once I am freed on Sept 1," she said at Tapah Prison yesterday.
Prisons Department deputy commissioner-general (Rehabilitation) Hafidz Othman launched the programme.
Another inmate, Angah (not his real name), 36, described the licensed release as an opportunity to celebrate Hari Raya Aidiladha with his family after missing   Aidilfitri.
Angah, who is serving a sentence for drug-related offences, is determined to leave behind his bad habits for the sake of his daughter, 10,  and his ageing parents.
"I feel very guilty for neglecting my responsibilities towards my daughter. I am grateful that my family still accepts me, and I will use this opportunity to help   my father's food business and rebuild a stable life."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Senior citizens bill to be tabled soon'
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
THE government aims to table the Senior Citizens Bill in the next Parliament session.
Women, Family and Community Development Minister Datuk Seri Nancy Shukri said stakeholders were being consulted before it is finalised.
"The bill is expected to include provisions on parental care responsibilities and the welfare of senior citizens to address the ageing population. We are targeting the next Parliament session because the draft is ready."
She said it was now subject to procedural matters and further review by the Attorney-General's Chambers (A-GC).
Nancy was speaking after the launch of the Malaysian Care Ecosystem Seminar and Stakeholder Consultation yesterday.
The event titled "Assuring the Quality of Caregivers", was organised by the ministry, the National Council of Women's Organisations Malaysia and  Universiti Malaya's Faculty of Medicine at the Kuala Lumpur Convention Centre.
She also said a bipartisan parliamentary select committee was overseeing discussions on the bill.
On the rising elderly care costs, she said the government provided tax relief for children caring for elderly parents at home.
She added that the government was studying proposals to help families who placed elderly parents at private care homes.
The ministry also plans to provide professional training for Welfare Department officers, affiliates and external providers to improve caregiving standards.
In her speech earlier, Nancy said Malaysia was expected to become an ageing nation by 2040, when more than 15 per cent of the population will be    65 and older.
She said as of last year, the elderly population surpassed 2.9 million.
Falling fertility rates, smaller households and rising female participation in the workforce continue to drive the demand for long-term care and community-based caregiving services.
"This year, the ministry targets to train 2,000 caregivers, including Welfare Department officers, caregivers for bedridden individuals and community-based providers," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Zeti calls for talent overhaul to secure nation's growth
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Former Bank Negara governor Tan Sri Dr Zeti Akhtar Aziz said Malaysia's competitiveness hinges on its ability to overhaul talent development for a volatile global economy.
Speaking at the FLUX Business Forum, Zeti said talent management is vital in building long-term economic resilience, especially as businesses face growing uncertainty from geopolitical tensions, supply chain disruptions and technological change.
She added that a more skilled workforce would help Malaysia improve productivity, create new opportunities and strengthen the country's competitiveness.
Zeti stressed the need for the educational system to adapt to the changing demands of the economy, as workers today require different skills compared with previous decades.
She also emphasised the importance of economic flexibility, noting  that it depends on having the right talent to help businesses and institutions adjust to changing conditions.
Businesses, policymakers and financial institutions must remain agile and continue adapting to new realities instead of relying on outdated systems and ways of thinking, Zeti added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: WHO: Ebola outbreak to grow
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
THE Ebola outbreak linked to more than 130 deaths in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo likely started two months ago and is expected to continue to grow, the World Health Organisation said on Wednesday.
The outbreak of the rare Bundibugyo strain, for which there is no vaccine, was declared last Friday and has alarmed experts because of how long it went undetected while spreading across a densely populated area, making it difficult to trace and isolate the contacts of infected individuals.
The WHO had pointed to "a critical four-week detection gap" between when the first known case started showing symptoms and laboratory confirmation of the outbreak.
"Investigations are ongoing to ascertain when and where exactly this outbreak started," Anais Legand, WHO technical officer for viral threats, said.
"Given the scale, we are thinking that it has started probably a couple of months ago."
The first suspected death was reported on April 20, and experts believe that was followed by a super-spreading event or location — either a funeral or a healthcare facility.
Six hundred suspected cases and 139 suspected deaths have been recorded so far, WHO director-general Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus said here. Fifty-one cases have been confirmed by laboratory testing in Congo and two cases have also been confirmed in neighbouring Uganda.
Tedros said a WHO Emergency Committee had met on Tuesday and confirmed the outbreak was a public health emergency of international concern but not a pandemic emergency.
He had declared the emergency at the weekend, the first time a WHO chief had taken that step without first consulting experts, due to the urgency of the situation.
The Bundibugyo strain of Ebola that caused the outbreak has an average fatality rate of around 40 per cent, according to the WHO.
WHO experts have said that two possible vaccines are under consideration for use in the outbreak but could take three to nine months to be developed.
An American doctor who was working in Congo is among the confirmed Ebola cases.
He has been transferred to Germany and his family are being taken to an isolation ward in the same hospital, Germany's health ministry said on Wednesday.
The US government said it had mobilised an initial US$13 million to respond to the outbreak and would help open 50 clinics to treat Ebola cases.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Kajang Prison's famous 'karipap pusing' draws crowds - and jokes online
Article:
KAJANG: Did you know that you can buy karipap from Kajang Prison?
The little-known fact recently became a viral talking point after a Threads user, @Kikinnnb_, asked whether the public was allowed to enter the prison compound to buy the much-talked-about 'karipap pusing' or if an appointment was required beforehand.
The post quickly caught the public's attention online, especially after the Prisons Department's official account responded in a light-hearted manner.
"You can simply walk into Kajang Prison and inform personnel at the guard post that you wish to go to Prismart Kajang Prison for the mutton curry puffs, or visit the My Pride Sales Gallery to shop for batik fabric, woven songket and frozen food products made by prison inmates.
"And if the curry puffs are delicious, don't go sharing it around, later there might not be enough," the reply read.
The humorous response delighted social media users and further fuelled public curiosity over Kajang Prison's curry puffs, which are known to sell out quickly almost every day.
Kajang Prison's famed "karipap pusing" are especially popular for their flaky spiral pastry and generous fillings.
Among the most sought-after flavours are chicken, beef and mutton, apart from the more conventional potato filling.
Many online users claimed the mutton curry puff was the hardest to get due to overwhelming demand.
The viral exchange also sparked a flood of jokes in the comments section.
One user jokingly suggested that @Kikinnnb_ spend "two or three years inside Kajang Prison" to learn the secret recipe behind the famous curry puffs.
Others joked that the prison curry puffs had become so famous that they deserved their own Michelin review, while some claimed the snacks were "worth getting detained for".
Beyond the humour, many users praised the initiative by the Prisons Department, saying programmes involving baking, cooking and handicraft production provide inmates with valuable vocational skills and rehabilitation opportunities.
Apart from the famous curry puffs, visitors can also purchase other products made by inmates, including frozen food, batik fabrics and woven songket items at the prison's Prismart outlet and My Pride sales gallery.
For many Malaysians, however, the real star remains the prison's flaky "karipap pusing", a snack so popular that even the Prisons Department jokingly warned customers not to tell too many people about it for fear supply would run out.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Brain drain deepens [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's brain drain reflects deeper structural issues in its wage system, career mobility and economic model as professionals continue to seek better opportunities overseas amid limited high-value jobs at home, an economist said.
The outflow of talent points to weaknesses in Malaysia's ability to create globally competitive industries, retain innovation and provide long-term career pathways for highly educated workers.
Associate Professor Dr Nik Ahmad Sufian Burhan, head of social and development sciences at Universiti Putra Malaysia's Faculty of Human Ecology, said Malaysia has yet to create enough high-value and high-skilled jobs to retain talent.
"The concern is not only whether people have jobs, but whether those jobs offer strong wages, serious career development and work that builds advanced capability," he told 'Business Times'.
He was responding to the World Bank's Malaysia Economic Monitor April 2026 report that more than half of the 1.86 million Malaysians living overseas are employed in skilled and semi-skilled occupations.
Preferred destinations for Malaysian talent include Singapore, Australia, the United Kingdom and the United States, where skilled workers are recruited through skills-based migration policies and offered stronger wages, better career progression and more advanced innovation ecosystems.
The report said that while Malaysia continues producing graduates and technically trained workers, the domestic economy has struggled to absorb highly skilled talent into high-productivity and high-paying roles.
At the same time, Malaysia remains dependent on low-skilled foreign labour, effectively making the country a net exporter of skills.
Nik Ahmad Sufian said many young professionals find themselves in positions that underuse their qualifications or provide limited opportunities for advancement, while foreign economies offer clearer links between skill, reward and opportunity.
He said brain drain should be understood as a structural gap rather than a narrow policy issue.
"If wages remain modest, promotion is slow and many firms compete mainly on cost, skilled workers will naturally look elsewhere," he said.
He added that Malaysia has invested heavily in education, but now needs to strengthen domestic firms and build industries that reward knowledge, creativity and technical expertise.
According to the World Bank, signs of talent migration are also becoming visible in Malaysia's innovation landscape.
The report said Malaysian inventors are contributing more patents to foreign-owned firms, while patents owned by Malaysian applicants have remained largely stagnant over the years.
The share of patents involving Malaysian inventors owned by local entities fell from more than 60 per cent in the early 1990s to below 20 per cent by 2020.
At the same time, foreign-owned patents involving Malaysian inventors consistently outperformed locally owned patents in terms of global quality indicators.
Nik Ahmad Sufian said the trend reflects gaps in Malaysia's domestic innovation ecosystem rather than a lack of local talent.
He said foreign firms often possess deeper research funding, stronger laboratories, clearer intellectual property strategies and better commercialisation pathways, making them more attractive to highly skilled researchers and inventors.
"Many domestic firms lack the scale, capital and risk appetite needed to turn ideas into patents, products and global revenue," he added.
He said if Malaysia continues losing skilled professionals and innovators overseas, the country risks becoming a supplier of talent to other economies while struggling to move into higher-value industries at home.
He said the long-term consequences extend beyond labour shortages, affecting productivity growth, research capacity, managerial quality and investor confidence.
"The greater danger is a cycle where limited high-end opportunities push people abroad, and the loss of talent then makes it harder to create those opportunities locally," he said.
He said talent retention policies alone would not be enough unless accompanied by economic transformation.
"The most effective talent policy is to create better opportunities here, through stronger firms, higher productivity, merit-based advancement and a more ambitious innovation ecosystem that rewards excellence at home," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Managing the trepidation of new projects at work
Article:
Every new project at work arrives with a mixture of opportunity and uncertainty. Even experienced professionals can feel uneasy when stepping into unfamiliar territory. A new assignment often brings different expectations, new people, tight deadlines, and the possibility of failure. While excitement may exist, trepidation frequently sits alongside it. This emotional response is natural and common in workplaces everywhere.
The beginning of a project can feel overwhelming because there are often more questions than answers. People may worry about whether they possess the right skills, whether they will meet expectations, or whether unexpected obstacles will appear later. The mind naturally imagines worst-case scenarios before progress has even begun. This can lead to hesitation, self-doubt, and anxiety.
One of the main reasons new projects create fear is the disruption they bring to routine. Most people feel comfortable when they understand their tasks and environment. Familiarity creates confidence. A new project removes that sense of certainty and replaces it with ambiguity. Suddenly, individuals must learn quickly, adapt rapidly, and operate without complete clarity. This discomfort can make even capable employees feel vulnerable.
New projects are often closely watched by managers, colleagues, or clients. Employees may feel that their performance will define how others judge their competence. People may overthink decisions or delay action because they fear making mistakes.
However, trepidation is not always negative. In many cases, it reflects a sense of responsibility and care. A person who feels nervous about a project is often someone who genuinely wants to do well. The problem only arises when fear becomes stronger than action. Managing these emotions is therefore essential for both personal wellbeing and professional success.
The first step in dealing with project-related anxiety is acknowledging it honestly. Many workplaces encourage confidence and decisiveness, which can make employees reluctant to admit uncertainty. Yet suppressing fear rarely removes it. Recognising anxiety as a normal response helps reduce its power. It allows individuals to approach challenges more rationally instead of emotionally.
Preparation is one of the most effective ways to reduce fear. Breaking a large project into smaller tasks can make it feel far more manageable. Instead of focusing on the entire workload at once, individuals can concentrate on completing one stage at a time. Small achievements gradually build confidence and momentum.
Next, communication plays a critical role. People sometimes struggle silently because they fear appearing incapable. In reality, asking questions early often prevents larger problems later. Most successful projects depend on collaboration rather than isolated effort. Seeking support should therefore be viewed as a strength rather than a weakness.
Another important factor is accepting that mistakes are inevitable in unfamiliar situations. Perfectionism can become a major obstacle during new projects. Growth usually involves setbacks, adjustments, and moments of confusion. Viewing mistakes as opportunities to improve rather than evidence of failure creates a healthier mindset.
Confidence is also built through experience. The more individuals expose themselves to new responsibilities, the more resilient they become. A person who has successfully navigated difficult projects in the past is better able to manage future uncertainty. What once felt intimidating eventually becomes familiar.
A supportive workplace culture can make a significant difference as well. Environments where employees feel safe to share concerns encourage healthier responses to stress. Leaders who communicate openly, provide guidance, and remain approachable help reduce unnecessary anxiety. When employees feel trusted and supported, they are more willing to take initiative and embrace challenges.
Trepidation often increases when deadlines appear impossible or workloads feel disorganised. Establishing priorities and maintaining structure can prevent panic from escalating. Even simple habits such as keeping notes, setting realistic goals, and reviewing progress regularly can create a greater sense of control.
Maintaining perspective is equally important. At the start of a demanding project, it can feel as though every decision carries enormous consequences. In reality, most workplace challenges are temporary and manageable. A setback on one project does not define an entire career. Remembering this can reduce unnecessary pressure and help individuals approach difficulties with greater calmness.
Personal wellbeing should not be ignored during stressful periods. Anxiety tends to worsen when people neglect rest, exercise, or balance in their lives. Long hours and constant worry may initially seem productive, but exhaustion eventually reduces focus and confidence. Taking care of physical and mental health allows individuals to think more clearly and respond more effectively to challenges.
Importantly, new projects are often where the greatest professional growth occurs. While familiar tasks provide stability, unfamiliar assignments create learning opportunities. They force people to develop new skills, improve communication, strengthen resilience, and expand their capabilities. Many individuals later realise that the projects they feared most became the experiences that shaped them positively.
Ultimately, trepidation in the face of new workplace projects is a deeply human response. It reflects uncertainty, responsibility, and the natural fear of failure. Yet these emotions do not have to become barriers. Through preparation, communication, adaptability, and perspective, individuals can move beyond fear and engage confidently with new challenges.
Every successful project begins with uncertainty. The difference between those who grow and those who remain stuck is not the absence of fear, but the willingness to continue despite it.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Goldman Sachs to pay US$500mil to settle shareholder lawsuit over 1MDB scandal
Article:
NEW YORK: Goldman Sachs has agreed to pay US$500 million to settle a class action lawsuit accusing the Wall Street bank of defrauding shareholders about its work for 1MDB, a Malaysian sovereign wealth fund embroiled in a corruption scandal.
The parties said last month they had agreed to settle, but the terms were not disclosed at the time. Lawyers for the shareholders, led by Swedish pension fund Sjunde AP-Fonden, disclosed the amount in a Wednesday filing in Manhattan federal court.
"The settlement is an outstanding result for the class," lawyers for the plaintiffs wrote in the filing.
A judge must approve the settlement.
Neither Goldman Sachs' lawyers nor a spokesperson for the bank immediately responded to requests for comment.
Former Malaysian prime minister Datuk Seri Najib Razak set up 1MDB to promote economic development, with help from Malaysian financier Jho Low, who is now a fugitive.
US and Malaysian authorities have said US$4.5 billion was siphoned away from 1MDB, with some diverted to offshore bank accounts and shell companies linked to Low.
Goldman helped 1MDB sell US$6.5 billion of bonds and collected an estimated US$600 million in fees.
Shareholders accused the bank of lying about its role in the fraud while repeatedly touting its supposedly robust risk management.
They said Goldman's share price tumbled after investors realised Goldman "actively facilitated - and handsomely profited from" the fraud.
Goldman agreed in 2020 to pay US$2.9 billion in penalties and have a Malaysian unit admit criminal wrongdoing to settle 1MDB probes by the US Justice Department and other authorities.
A Brooklyn judge formally ended the US criminal case against Goldman in May 2024, after the bank completed a three-year deferred prosecution agreement.
One Goldman banker was convicted of helping loot 1MDB, while another pleaded guilty.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two detained over deadly helium cylinder blast in Sungai Petani
Article:
SUNGAI PETANI:  Two men in their 20s and 40s have been detained to assist investigations into a helium gas cylinder explosion at an expo site, which claimed the
life of a trader
near a supermarket here yesterday.
Kuala Muda district police chief ACP Hanyan Ramlan said investigations are still in the early stages to determine the cause of the incident, and police are working to identify the owner of the cylinder.
He said the site will be temporarily closed to allow investigations to proceed.
"We are conducting investigations at the scene, so we ask all traders to temporarily cease operations. Once completed, we will hand the site back to the organiser to continue the event," he said at a press conference  last night.
At the same time, Hanyan urged visitors who sustained injuries to come forward and report to the authorities.
Elaborating further, Hanyan said the explosion caused debris to be flung as far as 100 metres, striking several victims.
"Some of the fragments were hurled up to 100 metres from the cylinder and struck a man who was operating a business nearby.
"The victim sustained severe injuries to his right arm and the right side of his chest and was pronounced dead at the scene," he said.
He added that the case is being investigated as a sudden death report (SDR) and under Section 304A of the Penal Code.
It was reported that a man was killed while three others were injured in the explosion involving a helium gas cylinder at the expo site.
The deceased was identified as Azmi Ahmad, 42, while the identities of the injured have yet to be confirmed.
Meanwhile, Sungai Petani Fire and Rescue Station chief and operations commander, Senior Fire Superintendent II Siti Norafiza Mohd Noh, said the injured victims included a 15‑year‑old boy and two men aged 36 and 49.– BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Gaza healthcare, aid systems 'decimated', NGOs warn at UN
Article:
UNITED NATIONS: More than six months after the United Nations endorsed a peace plan in Gaza, the humanitarian situation there remains catastrophic, three global NGOs said Thursday, calling on Israel to respect its obligations.
Major gaps remain between Israel's commitments and the reality facing Palestinians, representatives for Oxfam, Save the Children and Refugees International told reporters at UN headquarters in New York.
"Israel continues to deny most experienced aid groups from bringing in essential supplies, like pipes to fix water systems, shelters, materials and medical supplies at the levels needed," Oxfam America President Abby Maxman said.
"This is despite promises of reconstruction, economic development, and long-term prosperity," she said.
Violence has also continued unabated, with relentless Israeli strikes, according to Teresa Soldner, an American surgeon who recently returned from Gaza.
"Trauma patients continued to arrive every single day that I was in Gaza," Soldner said. "I think that the Palestinian healthcare establishment has been absolutely decimated."
The UN Security Council adopted a resolution in November 2025 endorsing the US-backed peace plan, which called for the full resumption of humanitarian aid.
"Children are still turning up in our health clinics with severe acute malnutrition," said Janti Soeripto of Save the Children, adding the numbers rose from January to April.
With no educational system left, more than "600,000 children will be out of school for the third year running," Soeripto said.
Maxman added that a lack of sanitation and hygiene materials means families are exposed to disease from open sewage, and vital water and sanitation systems and services are still destroyed or in disrepair.
The ceasefire in Gaza officially came into effect on Oct 10, 2025, and the first phase of the truce saw the release of the last hostages seized in October 2023 in exchange for Palestinians detained by Israel.
The transition to the second phase – involving Hamas's disarmament and a gradual withdrawal of the Israeli army, which still controls more than half of the Gaza Strip – has been stalled.
Refugees International President Jeremy Konyndyk and the NGOs called on Israel to meet its obligations because the ceasefire deal "is failing, and it is failing because it is being made to fail."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US tightens border checks for World Cup [WATCH]
Article:
TUCSON: The United States is prepared for a travel surge  ahead of the World Cup, with officials saying visa and passport security screening has been tightened.
Diplomatic Security Service (DSS) regional security officer Jeffrey Kraus said the agency was working closely with consular officers, investigators and overseas missions to identify fraudulent travel documents and questionable visa applications.
"There is obviously a risk of visa fraud. A lot of our work in relation to the World Cup is focused on ensuring the security of the US and  foreign teams," he said during a briefing here.
[[nid:1445499]]
The World Cup will be held  from June 11 to July 19.
Kraus said the priority was to ensure legitimate travel continued smoothly.
He said agents would investigate suspicious cases flagged by visa officers, including forged passports, fake supporting documents and organised attempts to exploit the visa system.
Kraus said passport and visa fraud was often linked to broader transnational criminal networks.
However, he declined to discuss specific cases, saying the focus remained on identifying fraud  and stopping suspicious applications.
He said the DSS did not determine visa policy but worked closely with consular affairs teams responsible for visa issuance and fraud prevention.
"We get involved when there's a problem," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US voices hope for Iran deal progress as Pakistan army chief heads to Teheran
Article:
TEHERAN: US Secretary of State Marco Rubio on Thursday voiced hope of progress on ending the war with Iran, with mediator Pakistan's army chief poised for talks in Teheran.
The role played by Field Marshal Asim Munir, a powerful figure with a growing role in Pakistan's foreign relations, has taken on increased significance as US President Donald Trump warned that negotiations to end the war were on the "borderline" between a deal and renewed strikes.
"I believe the Pakistanis will be travelling to Teheran today. So hopefully that'll advance this further," Rubio told reporters on Thursday.
A ceasefire on April 8 halted the war launched weeks earlier by the United States and Israel, but negotiation efforts have so far failed to yield a lasting peace agreement.
A war of words has replaced open conflict but the impasse continues to weigh on the world economy, leaving everyone from investors to farmers in painful states of uncertainty.
On Thursday, Iran's ISNA news agency said Munir's visit was aimed at continuing "talks and consultations" with Iranian authorities, without providing details. Other Iranian media carried the same report.
Pakistan hosted in April the only direct negotiations between US and Iranian officials to take place since Feb 28, the day the war began.
Munir was at the centre of the action during that round of talks, greeting both delegations on their arrival and displaying remarkable bonhomie with US Vice President JD Vance.
But the talks ultimately failed, with Teheran accusing Washington of making "excessive demands."
Since then, the two sides have exchanged multiple proposals, with the threat of renewed war looming all along.
"It's right on the borderline, believe me," Trump told reporters Wednesday. "If we don't get the right answers, it goes very quickly. We're all ready to go."
He said a deal could come "very quickly" or "in a few days", but warned Teheran would have to provide "100 per cent good answers."
Rubio also criticised Nato allies for their refusal to help Trump's war against Iran.
"He's not asking them to commit troops. He's not asking them to send their fighter jets in. But they refuse to do anything," he said.
"We were very upset about that."
- 'FORCEFUL RESPONSE' -
Teheran's chief negotiator Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf on Wednesday accused Washington of seeking to restart the war, warning of a "forceful response" if Iran were to be attacked.
"The enemy's movements, both overt and clandestine, show that despite economic and political pressure, it has not abandoned its military objectives and is seeking to start a new war," Ghalibaf said.
Iran's foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei said the Islamic republic was examining points received from Washington, while repeating Teheran's demands for the release of its assets frozen abroad and an end to a US naval blockade.
Trump is under political pressure at home as energy costs rise.
The ceasefire halted the fighting but has not reopened the Strait of Hormuz, the vital waterway that normally carries about a fifth of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas.
The future of Hormuz remains a key sticking point in negotiations, with fears growing that the global economy will feel more pain as pre-war oil stockpiles deplete.
Iran imposed a Hormuz blockade as part of its retaliation in the war, allowing only a trickle of vessels through in recent weeks while introducing a toll system.
Iran's new body overseeing Hormuz said its claimed area of control extends to Emirati waters, drawing a sharp rebuke from Abu Dhabi.
Relations between Iran and the United Arab Emirates have been severely strained since the war, after Teheran launched missile and drone strikes against Gulf countries in response to US-Israeli attacks.
Hormuz carries around a third of global fertiliser shipments, raising concerns of higher food prices and shortages if the closure drags on.
- LEBANON STRIKES -
On another front in the war, Lebanese state media reported that an Israeli strike on Thursday damaged a hospital in Lebanon's south.
Since a truce began on April 17, Israel has continued to launch strikes, carry out demolitions and issue evacuation orders in south Lebanon, saying it is targeting Hizbollah, which has also kept up attacks.
Lebanon's health ministry said Israeli attacks have killed at least 3,089 people in Lebanon since March 2.
Hizbollah drew Lebanon into the Middle East war with rocket fire at Israel in retaliation for the killing of Iran's supreme leader in US-Israeli strikes.
The United States on Thursday sanctioned nine Hizbollah-linked individuals it accused of "obstructing the peace process in Lebanon."
"Hizbollah is a terrorist organisation and must be fully disarmed," said Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent.  --AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'They treat their animals better': Gaza flotilla activist alleges abuse by Israeli forces
Article:
ISTANBUL: Julien Cabral has just completed his first voyage with the "Flotilla for Gaza." One of his eyes is ringed with purple, he has a wound on his left temple and an injured shoulder blade.
The 57-year-old Belgian originally from the port of Antwerp was on board a small boat that left Turkiye with six other people – an Italian, another Belgian, a Malaysian, a Finn, a Canadian-Palestinian and a South African.
About 10 members of the Israeli forces intercepted them in international waters more than 500km from the Israeli coast on Monday, he said as he arrived at Istanbul airport on Thursday.
Turkiye has evacuated 422 people on special flights, including 85 of its own citizens, and deployed doctors and ambulances.
"They first jammed communications then boarded in broad daylight with guns and fired plastic bullets just for fun," said Cabral.
"We found out we were the 12th ship intercepted.
"We were surprised. There were corvettes all around us. They moved in with a lot of violence even though we all had our hands in the air."
Cabral said the Israelis were marine commandos. "We know them," he added.
- 'PUNCH TO THE TEMPLE' -
"I was the second in command on the boat. Our captain, an Italian, was still standing and they targeted him straight away. I took a punch to the left temple," Cabral recounted.
"Then they transferred us, still using violence, our hands bound with plastic ties onto this prison-ship, into containers.
"I heard them say in English, 'let's have some fun'."
Cabral said the detainees asked to see a doctor for three days but were always told "later, later."
"They confiscated the medication of one person who suffers from epilepsy. On board the boat 'Sirius', seven people had 35 fractures between them," he added, pointing to his ribs and arms.
On board, the soldiers threw down from the upper deck boxes filled with bread and water "but not in sufficient quantity", he said.
"There must have been 200 of us... We asked for more water, toilet paper, tampons for the women. We had to ask for everything."
The detainees were taken off the ship on Wednesday and taken in cramped prison vans to be locked up near Ashdod, in southern Israel.
Cabral said the handcuffs were "far too tight" and the detainees were bent double for hours on end.
"We couldn't see anything. They pressed on our necks," he said.
"And they kept on slapping us, insulting us... People were laughing with them, playing the Israeli national anthem. They were particularly harsh with the Jordanians, the Tunisians."
On Thursday morning, they were finally transferred to Ramon airport near Eilat, in Israel's south.
"There again, we were subjected to insults."
- "WE'RE GOING TO CARRY ON' -
Cabral is hoping to return to Belgium on Friday after seeing one of the doctors waiting for the evacuees. He also said he intended to leave again on the next flotilla.
"Absolutely. We're going to carry on," he said.
Bilal Kitay, a Turkish national from Bingol, a mainly Kurdish city in eastern Turkiye, held his wife in his arms.
This was his second trip with the "Flotilla for Gaza", on board a boat with about 10 other activists.
He insisted that their interception by Israeli forces was "much, much more violent than the previous one" in April.
"They attacked us," he said. "Each of us was beaten, women and men... It's what Palestinians experience all the time," he said.
"Unfortunately, they treat their animals better. They alone consider themselves human," he said. Kitay is also planning to leave on the next convoy.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump sending 5,000 US troops to Poland
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump said Thursday he was sending 5,000 troops to Poland, after Washington said an earlier planned deployment had been called off amid pressure on Europe to fend for itself.
Trump said the move was based on his relationship with Poland's president Karol Nawrocki, a nationalist ally whom he said he was "proud to endorse" in elections that Nawrocki won last year.
"I am pleased to announce that the United States will be sending an additional 5,000 Troops to Poland," Trump said on his Truth Social network.
Trump did not give further details, but his comments appeared to refer to a deployment of several thousand US troops to Poland whose fate has been unclear for several days.
Last week US officials said the deployment of 4,000 US forces to Poland had been scrapped, the latest move by Trump's administration to slash troop numbers and punish Nato allies for not helping with the Iran war.
Then Vice President JD Vance said on Tuesday that the deployment of the 4,000 troops had been delayed, not canceled, adding that Trump had not made a "final determination."
Vance added however that Europe had to stand on its "own two feet," as Trump continues his push to get European allies to take more of the burden for their defense.
Trump has appeared determined to punish allies who have failed to back the Middle East war or contribute to a peacekeeping force in the crucial Strait of Hormuz waterway, which Iran has effectively closed.
The Pentagon announced at the beginning of May that Washington would pull 5,000 troops from Germany after Chancellor Friedrich Merz said Iran was "humiliating" the United States at the negotiating table.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AI angst rising among tech-savvy Gen Z
Article:
THE AI revolution is here and the boos are getting louder.
As artificial intelligence reshapes industries and markets around the world, a sense of dread is deepening among young "digital natives" now entering the workforce, fearful of the impact on jobs and daily life as ChatGPT, Claude and Gemini become household names.
In a speech this week, former Google CEO Eric Schmidt told graduating University of Arizona students that the impact of AI would be "larger, faster, and more consequential" than anything before.
"It will touch every profession, every classroom, every hospital, every laboratory, every person, and every relationship you have," he said as boos rang out even as he addressed anxieties about job security and an uncertain future.
How real those fears are was on show in an announcement by Standard Chartered on Tuesday that it will cut over 7,000 jobs and replace "lower-value human capital" with AI.
Many tech firms are also cutting staff, citing AI. Meta, which is installing tracking software on US-based employees' computers to train its AI model, is planning to lay off 10 per cent of its workforce globally starting this month.
Amazon.com has axed some 30,000 corporate jobs in recent months as it pushes AI and efficiency, while in February fintech firm Block cut nearly half its staff.
The Iran war is also softening hiring.
Schmidt acknowledged the young generation's fears and called them "rational," but just like the current top executives he painted the change and disruption AI was bringing as something inevitable that everyone needed to adapt to.
However, even as CEOs embrace AI, there have been signs of pushback: from Chinese courts, to unions at South Korean carmakers, Hollywood scriptwriters and India's film industry.
And perhaps the clearest sign of discomfort with the vision of the world offered by tech companies is the rising discontent among America's youth.
An April report from Gallup showed that a rising number of Generation Z - those born between 1997 and 2012 - were anxious or angry about AI, while those who said they were hopeful or excited by it had fallen sharply compared with a year earlier.
Nearly half of respondents said the risks of AI outweigh the benefits, while 15 per cent said it was a net positive, a much bleaker view than a year ago.
Most recognised the need to be AI-savvy but said it hindered deeper learning and creativity.
"Negative emotions have intensified over the past year," the report's authors wrote and noted that usage was starting to plateau.
"Young adults in the workforce are significantly more likely to view AI as a risk than a benefit."
The data did show that positive views of AI increased with the level of usage and decreased amongst those who used it less.
Schmidt's frosty reception followed other recent shows of anger at AI.
At the University of Central Florida on May 8, a real estate executive Gloria Caulfield was similarly heckled and booed over a commencement speech on AI.
"The rise of artificial intelligence is the next industrial revolution," she said as boos rang out, catching her off guard.
"What happened? OK, I struck a chord... Only a few years ago AI was not a factor in our lives." The room burst out in cheers.
* The writer is from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tengku Amir Shah confers Selangor medals on 151 recipients in conjunction with Sultan’s 80th birthday
Article:
SHAH ALAM, May 22 — The Raja Muda of Selangor, Tengku Amir Shah Sultan Sharafuddin Idris Shah, yesterday conferred Selangor medals on 151 recipients in conjunction with the 80th birthday celebration of the Sultan of Selangor, Sultan Sharafuddin Idris Shah.
At the ceremony, one recipient was awarded the Pingat Kecemerlangan Sukan Selangor (PKS), while 21 individuals received Pingat Pekerti Terpilih (PPT) and 26 others were conferred the Pingat Perkhidmatan Cemerlang Selangor (PPC) medals.
In addition, 87 individuals received Pingat Jasa Kebaktian (PJK), while 16 recipients were awarded Pingat Perkhidmatan Selangor (PPS) medals.
The second day of the investiture ceremony was held at Dewan Jubli Perak Sultan Abdul Aziz here, with the recipients coming from the districts of Hulu Selangor, Gombak, Hulu Langat, Kuala Langat, Kuala Selangor, Sabak Bernam and Sepang.
Also present were Selangor State Assembly Speaker Lau Weng San, State Secretary Datuk Ahmad Fadzli Ahmad Tajuddin, State Legal Adviser Mohamad Onn Abd Aziz, State Financial Officer Datuk Haniff Zainal Abidin, State Rural Development, Unity and Consumer Affairs Committee chairman Datuk Rizam Ismail, and State Women’s Development and Social Welfare Committee chairman Anfaal Saari. — Bernama

Title: Firefighters battle darkness, steep terrain and dead torch batteries to rescue five stranded surveyors in Kota Belud
Article:
KOTA BELUD, May 22 — Five men who were conducting land surveying work and became stranded across a river due to swift currents in Kampung Melangkap, here, on Wednesday, were rescued in a challenging operation that lasted until Thursday morning.
The Sabah Fire and Rescue Department (JBPM) Operations Centre received an emergency call at 4.48pm on Wednesday, prompting eight personnel from Kota Belud Fire and Rescue Station (BBP) to the scene, located approximately 35km away.
Station Chief Elge Bakuk said upon arriving at the main road near the location at 6.33pm, the firefighters trekked 5km through the jungle and along the riverbank before locating the victims at 12.45am.
He said the operation faced difficulties due to the hilly and steep terrain, as well as the lack of Government Integrated Radio Network (GIRN) coverage and mobile phone reception, which hampered communication throughout the operation.
The personnel’s torches also ran out of battery while the search and rescue operation was being conducted in the dark.
“All victims were successfully escorted out to a safe area at 5.50am before the operation was called off at 9.20am Thursday,” said Elge.
The operation involved a Fire Rescue Tender (FRT) with four personnel, an Emergency Medical Rescue Services (EMRS) vehicle with two personnel and a four-wheel-drive Toyota Vigo with two personnel.
Meanwhile, the Pamuntarian-Ulu Mosopoh road in Nabawan, which had been impassable since last Saturday due to flooding, is now open to all types of vehicles, reconnecting residents from six villages to Nabawan town.
Kampung Salarom Baru Village Chief Lumambai Antawor said since the road was submerged, no vehicles could pass through, leaving residents cut off and making it difficult to deliver food aid to the affected areas.
He said the situation also hampered efforts to deliver food supplies to residents.
The travel of teachers and students to two schools — Sekolah Kebangsaan (SK) Pamuntarian and SK Ulu Mosopoh — was also affected.
Last Tuesday, the Nabawan and Tulid assemblymen offices took the initiative to distribute food aid using an alternative method.
“The aid items were transported by vehicle to a certain point before being transferred to another vehicle on the other side of the flooded area. This required manual labour, with people having to wade through shoulder-deep water,” said Lumambai.
The immediate relief was contributed by Nabawan Assemblyman Datuk Abdul Ghani Mohamed Yassin and Tulid Assemblyman Jordan Jude Ellron.
The donations were delivered by Nabawan Community Development Leader Deckson Andipai, representing Abdul Ghani, while Antan Anginsor represented Jordan.
Meanwhile, residents’ representative Ambilek Omopor expressed gratitude to both elected representatives for the swift assistance provided while waiting for follow-up aid coordinated by the District Disaster Management Committee.
He said the committee is chaired by Nabawan District Officer Marshal Anthony @ Idham.
Ambilek, who is also Chairman of Kampung Salarom Baru Village Development and Security Committee (JKKK), said besides food aid, residents hope the Pamuntarian-Ulu Mosopoh road can be upgraded soon to resolve the issue of water overflowing during the rainy and flood seasons.
He added that there are several locations along the road that are frequently submerged and rendered impassable to vehicles every time a flood occurs. — Daily Express

Title: Perak MACC remands three over alleged false medical claims worth RM53,000
Article:
IPOH, May 22 — The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) in Perak has obtained a remand order against three individuals — a government department director, an executive officer and a company owner — over the submission of documents containing false particulars involving health treatment claims amounting to about RM53,000.
A source said a seven-day remand order until May 26 was issued against two suspects, a married couple in their 40s, by Magistrate A Hafiizh Abu Bakar after an application by the MACC at the Ipoh Magistrate’s Court on Wednesday morning.
“A five-day remand order until May 24 was also issued against another male suspect, a government department director in his 50s.
“All three suspects were detained between about 4.50pm and 9.30pm on Tuesday when they turned up to give statements at the Perak MACC office,” the source said.
Initial investigations found that the suspects were believed to have conspired in committing the offence between 2024 and 2025.
“The three suspects are believed to have submitted documents containing false particulars to a government agency to claim reimbursement for physical rehabilitation treatment expenses, although the treatment was never carried out,” the source added.
Meanwhile, Perak MACC director Azizul Ahmad Sarkawi, when contacted, confirmed the arrests and said the case was being investigated under Section 18 of the MACC Act 2009. — Bernama

Title: SNCC says Malaysian Gaza flotilla activist Haroqs receives alleged Mossad-linked threats
Article:
SEPANG, May 22 — The Sumud Nusantara Command Centre (SNCC) has confirmed that Global Sumud Flotilla (GSF) 2.0 activist Muhammad Hareez Adzrami, also known as Haroqs, has received threats believed to be linked to a proxy of the Israeli Zionist regime.
In this regard, SNCC director-general Datuk Sani Araby Abdul Alim Araby said that a number of security measures have been put in place for Haroqs, 26, and his mother.
“One of our participants, Hareez or Haroqs, was threatened via social media believed to be linked to a proxy of the colonialists.
“We have immediately informed the Government about this matter, as it involves not only his safety there but also the safety of his mother (in Malaysia),” he said at a press conference on the latest developments of the GSF 2.0 mission here yesterday.
He said Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim had assured that the safety of Haroqs and his family members would be safeguarded.
“The prime minister assured me that we (the government) would seek assistance from the Turkish authorities in monitoring (Haroqs’ safety), as Turkiye has intelligence capabilities and diplomatic relations,” he said.
Local media portals reported that Haroqs’ family had received threats believed to be linked to parties associated with Mossad.
The matter emerged after an X account known as Mossad Commentary uploaded a photograph and video of the activist in a post that later went viral, prompting reactions from Malaysian social media users.
